frameworks.name: convolutional neural networks
frameworks.one_line: CNNs as model class for hierarchical human visual cortex representations

frameworks.name: representational similarity analysis
frameworks.one_line: Compares CNN feature geometry against fMRI response geometry

frameworks.name: recurrent processing
frameworks.one_line: Tested whether recurrence narrows the CNN-brain gap

core_question: Do convolutional neural networks fully capture human visual cortex representations of real-world and artificial objects?
key_assumptions: RSA over fMRI responses indexes representational structure
Real-world and artificial visual stimuli engage overlapping neural circuits
CNN architecture/training choices span a meaningful model space
manipulations: Stimulus class: natural vs artificial images
Visual hierarchy level: lower vs higher cortical stages
Training regime and presence of recurrence
key_findings: CNNs match lower-level representations of real-world objects
CNNs fail to fully capture higher-level real-world representations
CNNs fail at both lower and higher levels for artificial objects
Failures persist across architecture, training, and recurrence
interpretations: CNN-brain correspondence is bounded and stimulus-class dependent
Current CNNs are not sufficient models of high-level human vision
